question: Explain the core principle of the Breadth-First Search (BFS) algorithm and describe its key properties.
expectedPoints: BFS traverses a graph level by level, exploring all neighbors at the present depth before moving on to nodes at the next depth level.
It uses a FIFO (First-In-First-Out) queue data structure to manage the order in which nodes are explored.
Key properties include completeness (will find a solution if one exists) and optimality for unweighted graphs (finds the path with the fewest edges).

question: List and briefly describe the main steps involved in executing the BFS algorithm on a graph, starting from a given root node.
expectedPoints: Mark the root node as visited and enqueue it.
While the queue is not empty, dequeue a node and process it.
Get all adjacent, unvisited neighbors of the dequeued node, mark them as visited, and enqueue them.
Repeat the process until the queue is empty.

question: Analyze the time and space complexity of the BFS algorithm. Discuss a practical advantage and a significant limitation of BFS in the context of AI problem-solving.
expectedPoints: Time Complexity: O(V + E), where V is the number of vertices and E is the number of edges, as each node and edge is processed once.
Space Complexity: O(V), in the worst case, the queue needs to store all nodes at one level of a balanced tree, which can be roughly O(V) for a broad graph.
Advantage: Guarantees the shortest path in an unweighted graph, which is critical for many AI applications like puzzle solving.
Limitation: High memory consumption, as it must store all expanded nodes at the current level, making it impractical for very large or infinite state spaces.
